questions.question: What is `/proc` on Linux?
options: A regular directory of log files
A pseudo-filesystem with no on-disk backing — reads materialize live kernel/process state into bytes; writes change kernel state
questions.explanation: /proc is virtual: nothing is stored on disk. When you `cat /proc/stat`, the kernel synthesizes the bytes on read. This is what every CLI process-inspection tool (ps, top, htop, free) reads under the hood.

questions.question: What does a cgroup actually do?
options: Tracks a set of processes and enforces collective resource limits (CPU, memory, IO, PIDs) on them
questions.explanation: cgroup = control group. A kernel-tracked set of PIDs with attached limits. Writing a PID into a cgroup's `cgroup.procs` moves it; setting `memory.max=100M` in that cgroup caps memory for the whole group. Docker, systemd, Kubernetes all set these up.

questions.question: What does a Linux namespace isolate?
options: A process's view of one specific global resource — its own PIDs, its own mounts, its own network, its own hostname, etc. — without affecting the kernel scheduler
questions.explanation: Namespaces partition kernel-visible global resources per-process. A PID namespace gives a process its own PID space (your PID 1 isn't the system's). A net namespace gives its own routing table. A mount namespace gives its own mount tree. Combining these is the foundation of containers.

questions.question: What's the minimum stack of kernel features that makes a 'container'?
options: Namespaces (pid/net/mnt/uts/ipc/user) + cgroup + a root filesystem (chroot or pivot_root) + optional seccomp
questions.explanation: A container = a process started inside a fresh set of namespaces, placed in a cgroup with resource limits, with its filesystem pivoted to an image. Optionally constrained by seccomp/SELinux. Docker, podman, containerd, runc are all just user-space orchestrators of these primitives.

questions.question: You see /proc/1234/maps shows a region marked `[heap]`. What's that?
options: The portion of the process's virtual address space that brk()/sbrk() / malloc has expanded into for dynamic allocations
questions.explanation: `[heap]` is the conventional name for the process heap — the region the libc allocator grows by calling `brk` / `sbrk` (or mmap for large allocations). `/proc/<pid>/maps` shows every memory region (text, data, heap, stack, mmap'd files, libraries) with its address range and protections. Crucial for diagnosing memory bugs.
